narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 59.6% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first attempt, which is above our threshold for approval. You have a solid profile overall, with an undergraduate GPA of 3.30 that's above the typical student's 3.21, an LSAT score of 40 exceeding the average of 36.70, upper-middle family income, and plans to attend full-time as a male White student—demographics that align closely with most applicants. However, your school decile ranks in Year 1 and Year 3, both at 9 compared to averages around 5.5 to 5.7, stand out as weaker spots, suggesting performance in the lower end of your classes. When the model evaluated your application, your Year 3 decile rank had the strongest influence, pushing the prediction toward expecting a bar failure. Right behind it, your Year 1 decile rank played a similar major role in tipping the scales that way. Your family income level came next in importance, also contributing toward the failure prediction more than it helped. Your LSAT score followed closely, influencing the outcome in the direction of failure despite being above average for applicants. Your undergraduate GPA and racial background rounded out the top factors, each nudging the prediction further toward a fail risk. In contrast, your gender as male provided a slight counterbalance, leaning toward a pass prediction, while your full-time status had almost no impact. Compared to typical students, your high decile ranks place you below the middle of the pack academically, which outweighed your strengths in test scores and GPA for this model. To improve your chances next time, focus on boosting your academic decile ranks through stronger coursework and study habits, as those were the dominant factors here—perhaps by retaking undergrad classes or gaining relevant experience that demonstrates improvement. You might also consider LSAT prep to push your score even higher, since it still factored in significantly. With targeted efforts on grades and test performance, you could shift future predictions in your favor and strengthen your bar exam readiness. Keep pushing forward; many students turn things around with persistence.
